headline: Ancient Sturgeon Return to the Black Dragon River
meta_description: China and Russia release millions of juvenile fish to restore critically endangered sturgeon populations in the shared Amur River.
summary_short: After decades of overfishing emptied the Amur River of giant sturgeon, China and Russia are bringing the ancient fish back through coordinated breeding and release effor…
body:
In 2008, the BBC traveled to China's Heilongjiang province hoping to film the region's famous giant sturgeon—only to discover the river had been fished to emptiness. But as environmental priorities shifted across China, the two nations sharing the waterway resolved to act.

On the first Tuesday in July, environmental authorities from China and Russia gathered in Tongjiang city for a remarkable collaborative effort. They released 5 million juvenile fish into the Amur River, known as the Black Dragon River in Chinese. Among them were 200 juvenile Amur sturgeon and 485,000 kaluga, another sturgeon species native to the river. Remarkably, many of the sturgeons received fluorescent marker injections to track their lifespans and reproduction patterns—turning the river into a living research lab.

Progress is measurable. While previous decades of releases hadn't visibly restored populations, Chen Huaifa, director of the Heilongjiang Aquatic Animal Resources Conservation Center, reported something new: sturgeon now survive to five years and older—unrecorded just eight years prior. In 2025, the center tracked one sturgeon reaching eight years, the age when males first spawn.

For local fisherman Li Changyou, who has fished for decades, the annual releases feel like hope restored. "When there are more fish in the river," he told reporters, "fishermen can have a more secure future."
